characterized_levers.name: Technical Feasibility Strategy
characterized_levers.consequences: Demonstrating technical feasibility impacts policy. Immediate: Demonstrators built → Systemic: 15% increased policy traction due to concrete evidence → Strategic: Enhanced credibility in procurement negotiations.
options: Focus demonstrator efforts on a single, highly secure authentication method, such as FIDO2, on a mainstream Android device.
Develop demonstrators for both FIDO2 on GrapheneOS and a browser-based fallback, accepting slightly longer development timelines.
Explore passwordless authentication via blockchain-anchored identity and decentralized identifiers (DIDs) alongside FIDO2 and browser-based fallback demonstrators.
characterized_levers.review: Controls Technical Risk vs. Scope. Weakness: The options don't explicitly address the cost implications of each demonstrator approach.
characterized_levers.deduplication_justification: This lever focuses on demonstrating technical feasibility to impact policy, quantifying the impact. It is distinct because it quantifies the impact of the technical demonstrators on policy traction, unlike other levers. It also has specific options related to demonstrator development.
characterized_levers.description: The Technical Feasibility Strategy lever controls the scope and focus of the technical demonstrators. Its purpose is to provide credible evidence that platform-neutral and fallback-capable authentication is technically viable. Objectives include demonstrating FIDO2 on privacy-respecting Android and/or a browser-based fallback. Key success metrics are the completion of working demonstrators and their acceptance as technically sound by external reviewers. This lever directly impacts the credibility and persuasiveness of the project's advocacy efforts.
characterized_levers.synergy_text: This lever strongly enhances the Procurement Influence Strategy. Credible technical demonstrators provide concrete evidence to support specific procurement requirements for platform-neutral access. It also supports the EU Standards Engagement Strategy by providing practical examples.
characterized_levers.conflict_text: A broad technical scope can conflict with the Policy Advocacy Strategy if it dilutes focus and overcomplicates the message. Focusing on too many authentication methods may weaken the argument for a specific, viable fallback path. It also conflicts with budget constraints.

characterized_levers.name: Policy Advocacy Strategy
characterized_levers.consequences: Policy advocacy shapes procurement. Immediate: Policy proposals submitted → Systemic: 20% greater likelihood of influencing AltID requirements → Strategic: Increased adoption of platform-neutral standards.
options: Focus advocacy on continuity of service and supplier concentration risks, avoiding direct privacy arguments.
Balance continuity arguments with privacy and democratic accountability concerns, broadening the coalition.
Prioritize open-source governance and community-led development models for digital identity, framing platform neutrality as a public good.
characterized_levers.review: Controls Political Risk vs. Coalition Size. Weakness: The options don't consider the potential for regulatory capture by incumbent vendors.
characterized_levers.deduplication_justification: This lever focuses on policy advocacy shaping procurement, quantifying the impact. It is distinct because it quantifies the impact of policy advocacy on influencing AltID requirements, unlike other levers. It also has specific options related to advocacy focus.
characterized_levers.description: The Policy Advocacy Strategy lever determines the framing and messaging used to influence Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and other stakeholders. Its purpose is to reframe the issue as one of resilience, continuity, and supplier concentration, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. Success is measured by the formal response from relevant authorities and the inclusion of relevant language in official documents. This lever shapes the political landscape for the project.
characterized_levers.synergy_text: This lever works in synergy with the Coalition Building Strategy. A broader advocacy focus, including privacy, can attract a wider coalition of support. It also amplifies the Procurement Influence Strategy by providing compelling arguments for platform neutrality.
characterized_levers.conflict_text: Focusing too heavily on open-source governance might conflict with the Procurement Influence Strategy if it alienates potential vendors or is perceived as impractical. Prioritizing privacy arguments could also conflict with the goal of emphasizing continuity of service, potentially weakening the resilience framing.

characterized_levers.name: Coalition Building Strategy
characterized_levers.consequences: Coalition strength amplifies advocacy. Immediate: Stakeholder engagement increased → Systemic: 30% stronger advocacy position in procurement discussions → Strategic: Greater political pressure for platform neutrality.
options: Build a narrow coalition focused on security experts and contingency-planning stakeholders.
Expand the coalition to include civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, and academic researchers.
Actively recruit dissenting voices from within the MitID operator and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to foster internal debate.
characterized_levers.review: Controls Speed vs. Breadth of Support. Weakness: The options fail to address the potential for conflicting priorities within the coalition.
characterized_levers.deduplication_justification: This lever focuses on coalition building to amplify advocacy, quantifying the impact. It is distinct because it quantifies the impact of coalition strength on advocacy position, unlike other levers. It also has specific options related to coalition composition.
characterized_levers.description: The Coalition Building Strategy lever defines the scope and composition of the project's support base. Its purpose is to create a broad coalition spanning civil society, privacy advocates, researchers, and policymakers. Objectives include securing buy-in from key stakeholders and mitigating potential opposition. Success is measured by the size, diversity, and influence of the coalition. This lever provides political momentum for the project.
characterized_levers.synergy_text: This lever synergizes strongly with the Policy Advocacy Strategy. A broader coalition allows for a more diverse and impactful advocacy campaign. It also supports the EU Standards Engagement Strategy by providing a wider network for disseminating information and influencing policy.
characterized_levers.conflict_text: Actively recruiting dissenting voices could conflict with the Procurement Influence Strategy if it creates internal divisions or undermines the project's credibility with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. A very broad coalition may also dilute the focus on resilience and continuity.

characterized_levers.name: Procurement Influence Strategy
characterized_levers.consequences: Procurement influence drives adoption. Immediate: Platform-neutral language included in procurement documents → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through mandated supplier diversity → Strategic: Reduced long-term dependency on Apple and Google.
options: Focus on including general language about supplier concentration risk in AltID procurement documents.
Advocate for specific requirements for platform-neutral access and certified fallback authentication paths.
Propose a 'digital sovereignty bonus' in procurement scoring, rewarding vendors who demonstrably reduce platform dependency.
characterized_levers.review: Controls Specificity vs. Implementability. Weakness: The options don't account for the potential for vendors to game the procurement process.
characterized_levers.deduplication_justification: This lever focuses on procurement influence driving adoption, quantifying the impact. It is distinct because it quantifies the impact of procurement influence on scaling, unlike other levers. It also has specific options related to procurement language.
characterized_levers.description: The Procurement Influence Strategy lever focuses on shaping AltID procurement documents to include requirements for platform neutrality and fallback access. Its purpose is to ensure that future digital identity systems are not exclusively dependent on Apple or Google. Success is measured by the inclusion of relevant language in official procurement documents. This lever directly impacts the design and architecture of future systems.
characterized_levers.synergy_text: This lever is amplified by the Technical Feasibility Strategy, as credible demonstrators provide evidence to support specific procurement requirements. It also benefits from the Policy Advocacy Strategy, which provides compelling arguments for platform neutrality based on resilience and supplier concentration.
characterized_levers.conflict_text: Advocating for a 'digital sovereignty bonus' could conflict with the EU Standards Engagement Strategy if it is perceived as protectionist or incompatible with EU interoperability requirements. Focusing on very specific requirements might also conflict with the goal of securing general language about supplier concentration risk.

characterized_levers.name: EU Standards Engagement Strategy
characterized_levers.consequences: EU standards engagement creates broader impact. Immediate: Input provided to relevant EU bodies → Systemic: 10% increased alignment with European Digital Identity Wallet requirements → Strategic: Enhanced interoperability and reduced lock-in across Europe.
options: Monitor relevant EU bodies and technical working groups, providing occasional input on interoperability.
Actively engage with EU bodies, advocating for platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms in eIDAS2 and related standards.
Champion a 'sovereign identity sandbox' within the EU Digital Identity Wallet framework to test and validate platform-neutral authentication models.
characterized_levers.review: Controls Resource Allocation vs. Scope of Influence. Weakness: The options don't address the potential for EU standards to be influenced by large platform vendors.
characterized_levers.deduplication_justification: This lever focuses on EU standards engagement creating broader impact, quantifying the impact. It is distinct because it quantifies the impact of EU standards engagement on alignment with European Digital Identity Wallet requirements, unlike other levers. It also has specific options related to EU engagement strategies.
characterized_levers.description: The EU Standards Engagement Strategy lever aims to influence European Digital Identity Wallet standards to support platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms. Its purpose is to align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, and resilience. Success is measured by the inclusion of relevant language in EU standards and guidelines. This lever ensures long-term sustainability and broader impact.
characterized_levers.synergy_text: This lever benefits from the Technical Feasibility Strategy, as demonstrators can be used to showcase viable platform-neutral authentication models to EU bodies. It also synergizes with the Coalition Building Strategy, leveraging the coalition's network to disseminate information and influence EU policy.
characterized_levers.conflict_text: Championing a 'sovereign identity sandbox' could conflict with the Procurement Influence Strategy if it is perceived as too radical or incompatible with existing Danish procurement processes. Passive monitoring may also conflict with the need to actively shape EU standards to align with the project's goals.
